# Dylan McDermott - Detailed Professional Profile

## Overview

Dylan McDermott is a meteorologist and geospatial data professional based in West Des Moines, Iowa. He builds automated geospatial and weather-data workflows that turn scientific observations into reliable maps, analyses, and operational products, drawing on meteorological analysis, catastrophe research, geospatial data engineering, remote sensing, and field-sensing research.

## Current Role: PLRB - Meteorologist

Dylan is a Weather and Catastrophe Analyst at Property & Liability Resource Bureau (PLRB), beginning in 2025. He designs, builds, and maintains ArcGIS-based geospatial products for weather-event verification, catastrophe analytics, and insurance-facing decision support.

His work includes automated Python and ArcPy workflows that download, quality-check, process, and update daily datasets for ArcGIS Server. He builds claims-facing ArcGIS Experience Builder applications, custom widgets, and REST API integrations for hail, wind, hurricane, current-weather, and location-specific weather analysis. He also integrates radar, satellite, numerical-model, and observational data into recurring products for severe weather, precipitation, smoke, evacuation, earthquake, power-outage, and catastrophe analysis.

Dylan evaluates data quality, source limitations, uncertainty, and transparent methodology for operational weather products. He conducted an exploratory machine-learning study estimating convective wind gusts from meteorological observations and engineered predictors. His work contributed to PLRB's 2025 Esri Special Achievement in GIS Award.

## Corteva Agriscience - Research Associate

At Corteva Agriscience in 2024, Dylan built and operated field-sensing systems and independently developed Python and ArcPy workflows for data ingestion, quality control, spatial assignment to field and plot boundaries, analysis, visualization, and research delivery.

He co-developed the Smartstick, a wheeled platform that collected spatially referenced crop-canopy measurements, and built the downstream workflow that connected GPS records, geospatial point creation, plot assignment, and plot-level summaries across more than seven research sites, including Johnston, Dallas Center, Lubbock, and Viluco. Dylan compared selected field measurements with drone imagery and drone LiDAR to support crop-stress research; the enclosure's internal logging and cloud-transfer implementation were handled by others.

Other Corteva work included co-designing and building the Gold Standard automated weather station and antenna-based data relay system, co-building and operating a sixteen-chamber automated nitrous oxide soil-gas flux experiment, and building, maintaining, shipping, and tracking infrared radiometers deployed across the Midwest, California, Texas, and South America.

## Master's Thesis - Iowa State University

Dylan earned an M.S. in Meteorology from Iowa State University in 2024. His thesis examined how historical U.S. land-use change affected Midwest rainfall, surface fluxes, moisture transport, and mesoscale convective systems.

The research used WRF and Noah-MP simulations; geospatial land-surface datasets translated from CESM/LUMIP inputs; and analysis of ERA5, CESM, WRF, and observational precipitation datasets using Python, xarray, MATLAB, and NetCDF.

## Education

- M.S. in Meteorology, Iowa State University, 2024
- B.S. in Meteorology, Iowa State University, 2022

## Core Capabilities

- Meteorological analysis, catastrophe research, radar applications, numerical weather prediction, and remote sensing
- ArcGIS Pro, ArcGIS Enterprise, Experience Builder, ArcPy, GeoPandas, Rasterio, spatial and raster analysis
- Python, pandas, xarray, NetCDF, GRIB, GeoTIFF, automated data pipelines, quality control, REST API integration
- TypeScript, JavaScript, React, FastAPI, AWS, SQL, PostgreSQL, Git, GitHub, and Linux
